Sternal skin conductance shares some physiological underpinnings with hot flashes due to common sympathetic activity but lacks direct evidence establishing it as a reliable correlate of symptom onset or severity. No studies validate sternal skin conductance as a practical or sensitive biomarker for assessing individual hot flash experiences; thus, it remains unsuitable as a surrogate measure compared to self-reports which reflect true symptomatic experience.

Sternal skin conductance reflects general sympathetic activation associated with hot flashes but lacks direct evidence as a reliable surrogate for measuring actual hot flash frequency or intensity.
